coredata.dc:title: Sustainable kiwi fruit derived carbon dots architected WO3 (KF-CDs@WO3) thin films based flexible electrochromic devices with improved stability for next generation smart windows applications
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ahmad, Khursheed
Kim, Haekyoung
coredata.dc:description: The design and development of new advanced materials or technologies are essential for the construction of high performance smart windows for the next generation of energy efficient buildings. Tungsten trioxide (WO3) based flexible electro-chromic devices f-ECDs have received extensive attention for the development of next generation f-ECDs. Here, we demonstrated the design and fabrication of kiwi fruit peel derived carbon dots (KF-CDs) decorated WO3 films under benign approaches. Furthermore f-ECDs were developed and their performance have been examined in terms of transmittance and coloration efficiency. The amount of KF-CDs in to the WO3 was varied and optimized f-ECDs exhibited the excellent optical contrast (ΔT) of 83.74 %, coloration efficiency of 86.2 cm2/C. The optimized f-ECDs exhibited excellent cyclic stability of 25000th cycles and retained more than 80 % optical contrast compared to the initial performance. This improved stability may be attributed to the presence of strong hydrogen bonding between the functional groups of KF-CDs and WO3.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Kiwi fruit derived carbon dots
Sustainable carbon dots
Flexible electro-chromic device
Smart windows

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of WO3 and KF-CDs@WO3 films

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) [grant number NRF-2022R1A2C1005585] and Korea Innovation Foundation (INNOPOLIS) [grant number 2020-DD-UP-0278] funded by the Korean government (MSIT).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the KF-CDs, WO3, KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3, and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3.

SEM image of the fabricated (a) WO3, (b) KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, (c) KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3, and (d) KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3. TEM images (e,f) of KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3. EDX mapping image for C (g), W (h) and O (i) elements of the KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3.
EDX analysis of (a,b) WO3 and (c,d) KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3.
XPS survey spectrum of KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 sample (a). C1s (b), W4f (c) and O1s (d) XPS scan of KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 sample.
UV–vis transmittance spectra (bleached and colored state) (a) and optical contrast (b) of WO3, KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs.
Transmittance (%) verses time (s) curves (a,c,d) and ΔT value (b,d,f) of WO3, WO3, KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs at different applied voltages.

Schematic device structure (a) and bleached, colored and bending state of KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs (b). Transmittance (%) versus time (s) curves (c) and ΔT (%) value (d) of KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs with respect to cylic stability. Transmittance (%) versus time (s) curves (e) and ΔT (%) value (f) of KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs with respect to mechanical bending stability.
Comparison of CE, Tc, Tb, ΔT, cyclic and bending stability of KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs with recently reported works [51–62].

head.ce:title: Sustainable kiwi fruit derived carbon dots architected WO3 (KF-CDs@WO3) thin films based flexible electrochromic devices with improved stability for next generation smart windows applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yeungnam University, Gyeongsan 38541, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The design and development of new advanced materials or technologies are essential for the construction of high performance smart windows for the next generation of energy efficient buildings. Tungsten trioxide (WO3) based flexible electro-chromic devices f-ECDs have received extensive attention for the development of next generation f-ECDs. Here, we demonstrated the design and fabrication of kiwi fruit peel derived carbon dots (KF-CDs) decorated WO3 films under benign approaches. Furthermore f-ECDs were developed and their performance have been examined in terms of transmittance and coloration efficiency. The amount of KF-CDs in to the WO3 was varied and optimized f-ECDs exhibited the excellent optical contrast (ΔT) of 83.74 %, coloration efficiency of 86.2 cm2/C. The optimized f-ECDs exhibited excellent cyclic stability of 25000th cycles and retained more than 80 % optical contrast compared to the initial performance. This improved stability may be attributed to the presence of strong hydrogen bonding between the functional groups of KF-CDs and WO3.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In modern world, energy consumption is increasing which may be responsible for energy crisis and need immediate attention [1–4]. Fossil fuel based energy resources are limited and emits CO2 [5]. To prevent a further rise in the average global temperature, it is essential for humans to achieve a state where they are no longer emitting CO2 [5–7]. In order to accomplish this goal on a global scale, nations around the world have expedited the advancement of various energy devices and technologies, including those related to energy storage, energy conversion, and energy conservation [8–13].

Recently, smart windows which are a type of electrochromic devices (ECDs), have attracted the scientific community because of their adjustable optoelectronic features which includes reflectance, transmittance and absorbance [14–17]. Smart windows hold the capacity to decrease energy usage in buildings for both air conditioning and heating purposes by simulating the flow of solar heat [18–21]. The main characteristics of the next generation smart windows include dynamic light control, energy efficiency, Indoor desirable temperature, long-term stability, integration with smart home technology [15]. In brief, next generation smart windows should have high energy efficiency (should work on low voltage with high coloration efficiency), fast switching time (smart window should change the color state with in a very short period of time, generally within seconds) [16]. The smart windows can contribute to a more comfortable and healthier indoor environment by optimizing natural light levels, reducing glare, and controlling the amount of heat entering a building [17]. This can improve productivity, mood, and overall well-being of occupants [18]. Furthermore, recent advancements in EC devices have broadened their utility beyond efficient energy management [22]. These devices now encompass versatile energy-storage solutions, including electro-chromic super-capacitors and electro-chromic batteries [23]. The typical configuration of ECDs consists of multiple layers, namely a transparent conducting layer, cathodic (electro-chromic) layer, electrolyte, anodic layer, and another transparent conducting layer [24]. The active layers (electro-chromic and anodic layers) produce optical changes through a redox reaction that occurs alongside the movement of ions such as Li+, K+, Na+, Al3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, H+ and electrons [25].

In the past few years, a new generation of ECDs with enhanced flexibility has been widely studied to overcome the limitations of conventional ECDs in lightweight and portable technologies [26–28]. Flexible ECDs (f-ECDs) have various advantageous over conventional ECDs and may be employed in the development of data storage devices, flexible smart windows, anti-glare mirrors, sunglasses and displays [29]. However, there are still some major challenges which hindered the successful realization of f-ECDs. The cyclic stability is one of the major concern of f-ECDs for their potential applications at large-scale production or commercialization [30]. Thus, it is of great significance to enhance the cyclic stability of f-ECDs.

Electro-chromic layer plays significant role and various electro-chromic layers such as transition metal oxides, polymers and organic materials have been widely used in the fabrication of ECDs. In particular, a large number of electro-chromic materials (MnO2, Prussian blue-based materials, SnO2, WO3, MXene, and NiO etc) have been studied for the construction of smart window applications [31–39]. In particular, WO3 possess excellent electro-chromic and optical properties which makes it desirable and promising candidate for the fabrication of f-ECDs [40]. WO3 has received enormous attention as counter electrode material due to its cost-effectiveness, eco-friendly nature, and ease of synthesis [40]. Despite excellent electro-chromic properties, WO3 suffers from poor cyclic stability, and switching time [41]. Previous studied demonstrated that switching time has been improved by incorporating conductive materials with WO3 [41]. Unfortunately, cyclic stability remained a challenge for scientific community for long-term practical applications.

Carbonaceous materials are receiving great interest of the scientific community due to their excellent physiochemical properties such as large specific area, electrochemical stability, and high electrical conductivity [42]. Particularly, carbon nanotubes and graphene are widely used additives due to their favorable electronic conductance, excellent physical stability against volume and bending expansion, and dispersibility in active materials [43]. More recently, several research studies highlights the potential versatility of carbon dots (CDs) in the form of nanocomposites for the development of electrochemical biosensors, Li-ion batteries, and electro-catalytic water splitting [44]. Thus, insertion of CDs to the WO3 structures may be beneficial to further enhance the stability of the f-ECDs. The synthesis of CDs using natural waste materials has gained much attention due to eco-friendly synthetic approach and recyclability of bio-waste materials [45,46]. Kiwi fruit is widely consumed fruit which is rich in vitamin C, dietary fibers, and antioxidants [46]. The kiwi fruit peel can be easily available as common food waste. Thus, it is of great importance to use the kiwi fruit peel as carbon source for the preparation of CDs.

In this paper, we demonstrated the synthesis of KF-CDs architected WO3 (KF-CDs@WO3) thin films by using kiwi fruit peel as carbon sources. Further, f-ECDs were fabricated by utilizing KF-CDs@WO3 as electro-chromic layer. The fabricated KF-CDs@WO3 based f-ECDs exhibited excellent cyclic stability of 25,000 cycles with bending stability of 2000 cycles. The introduction of KF-CDs may improve the electrical conductivity of the WO3, facilitate the charge transportation and presence of synergistic interactions between KF-CDs and WO3 may promote electrochemical reaction and enhance the EC performance of the fabricated ECDs. So far no report is available on the synthesis of KF-CDs@WO3 using kiwi fruit peel for the development of f-ECDs. This is the first research work which report the fabrications of highly stable f-ECDs using KF-CDs@WO3 as electro-chromic layer.

Tungsten powder (W powder) was bought from Alfa-aesar, South Korea. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2; 30 %) was purchased from Duksan, Korea. EL-72 was purchased from Adcro Company, South Korea. Kiwi fruit was purchased from SampungDong Home Mart shopping complex, Gyeongsan, South Korea. Liquid ammonia and all other used solvents and reagents were purchased from Merck and Sigma, South Korea.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of WO3 and KF-CDs@WO3 films
ce:section.ce:para: The KF-CDs were prepared using hydrothermal method. The kiwi fruit peel were dried at 60 °C for 6 h in vacuum oven to remove the moisture. Typically, kiwi fruit peel (30 g) was carefully crushed well in deionized (DI) water (50 mL) using grinding mixer. Then, 1 mL of aqueous ammonia was added to the obtained mucilage (50 mL) and transferred to Teflon reactor (capacity 100 mL) and covered with stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was tightened to avoid the melting of Teflon reactor. The autoclave was heated at 200 °C for 24 h. After completing reaction, autoclave was cool down to RT naturally. The obtained CDs was refrigerated at 4–5 °C for further studies. The synthetic procedure for KF-CDs has been described in Scheme S1.

The WO3 has been prepared as per the previous report with some modifications [47]. In brief, W powder (6.78 g) was carefully dissolved in 30 % H2O2 (93.2 g) with the help of magnetic stirring at room temperature (RT). The reaction is exothermic and ice bath has been used during the addition of W powder in the H2O2 solvent. This reaction mixture stirred at RT until clear solution was obtained. Further, this clear solution was stirred at 95 °C for 5 h and finally the solvent was evaporated to obtain the pale yellowish colored WO3. The obtained WO3 was grinded for 2 h using mortar and pestle. The synthetic approach for WO3 has been illustrated in Scheme S2.

For the fabrication of KF-CDs@WO3 thin films, KF-CDs@WO3 ink was prepared using sonochemical method. In brief, 1 g WO3 was added to the mixture of isopropanol (1.2 g) and DI water (1.2 g). Further, KF-CDs (0.5 wt%, 1.0 wt% and 1.5 wt%) were added to the WO3 dispersion and sonicated for 6–8 h. The WO3 ink was also prepared by the ultrasonication of 1 g WO3 in 1.2 g isopropanol and 1.2 g DI water for 6–8 h.

We have used X'pert Pro X-ray diffractometer to obtain the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the prepared samples. The SEM images of the prepared samples were checked on Hitachi, S-4800 scanning electron microscope (SEM). To evaluate the elemental composition and binding energy of the prepared composite material was checked on the Thermo-Scientific X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopic (EDX) images were obtained on Horiba instrument connected with Hitachi S-4800 microscope. Raman data was collected on Horiba Scientific spectroscope. In further studies, ZIVE SP10 electrochemical work station and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific; GENESYSTM 10 s; excitation wavelength of 700 nm) were utilized to obtain the UV–vis transmittance data.

In this study, we have fabricated f-ECDs using WO3, KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3 as electro-chromic (EC) layers. The prepared ink (WO3, KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3) was spin coated on to the surface of indium tin oxide coated polyethylene terephthalate (ITO/PET) substrates (active device area = 2.5 × 2.5 cm2). The thin films were fabricated at spin coating speed of 5000 rpm for 25 s and dried at 60 °C for 6–8 h. The polymer gel electrolyte (EL-72 with 10 % ferrocene) was deposited on the ITO/PET electrode. Finally, both the electrodes were assembled in the sandwich-like structure and laminated to develop the f-ECDs.

The XRD pattern of the hydrothermally synthesized KF-CDs was obtained to confirm the successful formation and crystalline nature of the KF-CDs. The obtained XRD pattern of the KF-CDs at the 2theta ranges of 5-80° has been presented in Fig. 1 . A broad diffraction peak was appeared between 20 and 30° which can be assigned to the (002) diffraction plane of KF-CDs [48]. This is suggested that KF-CDs are successfully formed on the hydrothermal treatment of kiwi fruit peel in presence of aqueous ammonia. The enlarged view of the KF-CDs has been provided in Fig. S1a. The Raman spectroscopy was also adopted to further verify the formation of KF-CDs. The Raman spectrum of KF-CDs is shown in Fig. S1b. The presence of D and G defects suggested the formation of KF-CDs and ID/IG ratio was found to be 1.04 (Fig. S1b) [49]. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was also utilized to observe the size of the prepared KF-CDs. Fig. S2 presents the TEM image of the prepared KF-CDs which is showing that CDs has particle diameter of less than 10 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was also used to further characterize and examine the presence of various functional groups and elemental composition in the prepared KF-CDs [48]. Fig. S3a presents the survey scan of the KF-CDs which demonstrated the binding energy for the presence of C1s and O1s. The high resolution XPS scan of C1s has been presented in Fig. S3b which exhibited the presence of C–C(sp2), C–C(sp3), C = O, and O = C-OH functional groups. The O1s spectrum of KF-CDs also revealed the presence of C-O and C = O bonds (Fig. S3c). The XPS results for KF-CDs are consistent with previous report [44]. The above aforementioned characterizations revealed that KF-CDs are formed using hydrothermal treatment. In addition, XRD pattern of the sol–gel synthesized WO3 has been demonstrated in Fig. 1. The XRD results exhibited the presence of various strong diffraction peaks which were well-matched with JCPDS Card Number 83–0950 [50]. The presence of well-known diffraction planes of (002), (200) and (202) in the XRD pattern of the WO3 authenticated the formation of WO3. The strong diffraction peaks suggested the presence of crystalline nature in the prepared WO3. Subsequently, we have also recorded XRD patterns of the KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3, and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3. Fig. 1 presents the XRD patterns of the fabricated KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3, and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3 films. The observed XRD results shows the presence of diffraction peaks related to the WO3 but KF-CDs could not be detected. This may be due to the poor crystalline/amorphous nature of the KF-CDs.

In further characterizations, SEM technique was utilized to examine and check the surface morphological characteristics of the fabricated thin films (WO3, KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3). The SEM picture of the WO3 has been depicted in Fig. 2 a. It can be noted that WO3 particles are agglomerated and formed the uniform distribution over the surface of ITO/PET, as shown in Fig. 2a. The SEM image of the KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3 exhibited the uniform distribution of the particles (Fig. 2b). Similarly, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3, and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3) exhibited the uniform distribution over the ITO/PET surface (Fig. 2c and Fig. 2d). The presence of KF-CDs in the prepared KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3, and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3) samples could not observed by the SEM technique. Thus, it is required to verify the presence of KF-CDs over the surface of WO3. In this regard, we have further used TEM approach to know the presence of KF-CDs in the KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 sample. The obtained TEM picture of the KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 sample are shown in Fig. 2e and Fig. 2f. The obtained results indicated the presence of KF-CDs in the prepared KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 sample. This authenticated the successful formation of KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 sample. The elemental mapping images of the C, W and O elements were also obtained. The elemental mapping image of C, W and O elements are presented in Fig. 2g, Fig. 2h and Fig. 2i, respectively. The elemental mapping images suggested the presence of C, W and O elements in the prepared KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 sample. It can be understood that KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 sample has been successfully formed. The elemental composition and phase purity of the WO3 and KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 samples were also determined by using EDX method. The obtained EDX spectrum of the WO3 is shown in Fig. 3 a which show the appearance of W and O elements without the appearance of other element related to the impurity.

Similarly, EDX spectrum of the KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 sample is shown in Fig. 3b. The observations clearly demonstrated the presence of C, W and O elements and revealed the formation of KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 sample.

In further characterization investigations, XPS technique was also adopted for the elemental composition and valence state studies of the KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3. The obtained XPS survey scan of KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 sample has been presented in Fig. 4 a. The survey scan of KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 sample show the presence of O1s, W4f, and C1s which suggested the formation of KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 sample (Fig. 4a). The C1s scan of KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 sample is presented in Fig. 4b. The C1s spectrum show the presence of C = O, C–C, and C-O functional groups. On the other side, W4f scan of KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 sample revealed the presence of W4f5/2 and W4f7/2 as shown in Fig. 4c. The W4f indicated the presence of W6+ and W5+ oxidation states which takes part in the coloring and bleaching process during the operation of f-ECDs. The O1s scan of KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 sample is depicted in Fig. 4d. The appeared peaks at 530.8, 531.6 and 532.9 eV can be assigned to the presence of lattice oxygen, O = C surface groups and chemisorbed oxygen species present at the surface of KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 sample. The obtained results are in well-agreement with previous study [50].

The performance of the fabricated f-ECDs were examined by obtaining their transmittance spectra. The transmittance data of the various fabricated f-ECDs are summarized in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5a show the lowest optical contrast (ΔT) of 68.23 % for WO3 based f-ECDs at wavelength of 700 nm. The KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs show the ΔT value of 72.92 % whereas KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs demonstrate the ΔT value of 79.19 % (Fig. 5a and Fig. 5b). The highest ΔT value of 83.74 % was observed for KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs (Fig. 5b). This suggested that KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs possess good electrochromic properties compared to the other f-ECDs.

Subsequently, electrochromic activities and performance of the WO3, KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs were evaluated by studying the change in the transittance on applying different voltages. The change in the transmittance was measured at measured wavelength of 700 nm. Fig. 6 a shows the obtained transmittance graphs of the WO3, KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs at applied voltage of −0.6 V. The observations show that WO3 based f-ECDs has the lowest ΔT value of 8.07 % at −0.6 V. The WO3, KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs shows the ΔT value of 9.52, 24.51, and 14.57 %, respectively (Fig. 6a and Fig. 6b). This demonstrate that KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs has highest ΔT value compared to the WO3, KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs. Similarly, we have further obtained the transmittance graphs of the WO3, KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs at applied voltage of −0.9 V. The obtained results are summerized in Fig. 6 and Fig. 6d. The observations show that KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs has highest ΔT value of 61.44 % compared to the WO3 (ΔT = 42.62 %), KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3 (ΔT = 44.63 %), and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3 (ΔT = 52.13 %) based f-ECDs (Fig. 6d). Further, transmittance curves of the WO3, KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs were obtained at applied voltage of −1.2 V and transmittance curves are displayed in Fig. 6e. It was observed that KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs has excellent ΔT value of 83.74 % which is higher than that of the WO3 (ΔT = 68.23 %), KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3 (ΔT = 72.92 %), and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3 (ΔT = 79.19 %) based f-ECDs (Fig. 6f).

The above investigations show that KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs has good electrochromic performance compared to the WO3, KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs. Therefore, we have selected KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 as potential electrochromic layer for further studies. The schematic representation of the fabricated f-ECDs is shown in Fig. 7 a. Fig. 7b shows the digital pictures of the KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs. From Fig. 7b, it can be clearly seen that on applying a voltage of −1.2 V, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs turns to blue. The bleached, colored and bending states of the KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs are shown in Fig. 7b which revealed that KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs has good bending properties. The performance of various fabricated f-ECDs can be further evaluated in terms of coloring time (Tc) and bleaching time (Tb). The Tb and Tc plays significant role and have been considered as most desirable characteristics for the development of eco-friendly smart windows in the practical applications. WO3 based f-ECDs shows the Tb and Tc of 40.3 s and 8.8 s, respectively. The KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3, and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs exhibited the Tb of 8.3, 16.37, and 17.1 s, respectively. On the other side, KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3, and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs exhibited the Tc of 11.3, 10.9, and 9.3 s, respectively.

The colorattion efficiency (CE) also an important feature of the f-ECDs for smart window applications. Thus, CE of the WO3, KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3, and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs were determined by employing equation (a) and equation (b) which are given below,
(a)
C E
cm
2
/ C
=
△ O D / △ Q
(b)
OD
=
l o g
T b
/
T c
(Herein, charge density is denoted as △Q and optical density is denoted as OD).

The calculated OD versus △Q graphs of the WO3, KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3, and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs are shown in Fig. S4a, S4b, S4c, and S4d, respectively. The CE of 52.9 cm2/C was found to be for WO3 based f-ECDs (Fig. S4a). Further improved CE of 63.2 cm2/C was observed for KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs (Fig. S4b). The signifiucant improvement in the CE value (86.2 cm2/C) was observed for KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs (Fig. S4c). The KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs exhibited the CE value of 70.46 cm2/C (Fig. S4d). The above observations showed that highest CE of 86.2 cm2/C was observed for KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs.

To study the role of the KF-CDs in the improved electrochromic performance of the KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was also applied. The Nyquist plots of the WO3, KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs were recorded. The obtained results are provided in Fig. S5 and Table S1. The observations suggetsed the presence of good conductive properties in the KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs compared to the other f-ECDs (Table S1). Moreover, charge transfer resistance (Rct) was lower in the KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs which may be due to the presence of reasonable synergism between KF-CDs(1.0 wt%) and WO3. Therefore, improved performance was observed for KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs.

The stability of the f-ECDs is also important characteristic and need significant attention. Hence, we have further study the cyclic stability of the KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs for the development of next-generation flexible smart window. The obtained results for the cyclic stability of KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs are shown in Fig. 7c. The KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs show the ΔT of 81.2 % after 1500 cycle whereas ΔT of 79.67 % was observed after 3000 cycles (Fig. 7d). The KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs demonstarte the ΔT value of 78.8 % after 6000 cycle whereas ΔT of 76.52 % was observed after 9000 cycles. The above observations suggested that KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs retained optical contrast of 91.37 % compared to the initial performance after 9000 cycles. In further studies, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs exhibited the ΔT of 68.78 % after 25,000 cycles (Fig. 7d). This indicated that KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs retained 82.4 % optical contrast compared to the initial performance after 25,000 cycles. Thus, we can say that KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 has good cyclic stability and retained more than 82 % optical contrast and can be used for the development of next-generation flexible smart windows.

It is believed that KF-CDs are the small carbon materials which can provide additional electro-active sites and may enhance the charge transport proeprties of the KF-CDs@WO3 based ECDs. This may lead to the more stable and efficient coloration/bleaching process during repeation of the cycles, making the ECDs more stable over time. The probable mechanism for the imprved stability has been described in Scheme 3 in the supporting information. In addition, it is expected that presence of carboxyl groups, hydroxyl and carbonyl groups form strong hydrogen bondng between the KF-CDs and WO3. The strong bonding may effectively tolerate the bending and long-term Li+ ion insertion/extraction cycles and improved the stability of the fabricated ECDs.

The study and examinations for flexible/mechanical stability of the f-ECDs is also important task. In this connection, we have also bended KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs up to 2000 cycles and its performance was evalaued at diffeernt bending cycle. The bending stability results are provided in Fig. 7e and 7f. The above observations show that KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs has ΔT of 74.26 % after 1000 cycles ΔT of 61.62 % after 2000 cycles (Fig. 7f). Thus, it is clear that KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs retained 88.67 % and 73.58 % of its initial performance after 1000 and 2000 bending cycles, respectively.

According to the recent studis, numerous ECDs have been reported using various electrochromic materials. The obtained reults in our present study are reasonable in terms of cyclic stability and bending stability compared to the recent studies as listed in Table 1 .

In this study, we can summarize that kiwi fruit peel derived CDs have been prepared. Further, KF-CDs@WO3 films were prepared under facile approaches. Further, flexible ECDs were fabricated which demonstrated excellent electro-chromic performance in terms of optical contrast and coloration efficiency. KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs possess high electro-chromic properties and showed highest performance compared to the WO3, KF-CDs(0.5 wt%)@WO3 and KF-CDs(1.5 wt%)@WO3based f-ECDs. In addition, KF-CDs(1.0 wt%)@WO3 based f-ECDs showed excellent cyclic stability of 25,000 cycles with 2000 bending cycles. This work offers the considerable prospects in the design and fabrication of next generation flexible smart windows or displays.
Khursheed Ahmad: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Haekyoung Kim: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) [grant number NRF-2022R1A2C1005585] and Korea Innovation Foundation (INNOPOLIS) [grant number 2020-DD-UP-0278] funded by the Korean government (MSIT).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2024.112546.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
